text.title: gateway-fallback-chain Failure Modes
text.summary: Four failure modes with symptom, root cause, and repair.

section.title: Antipatterns

antipattern.description: Fallback chain of same-vendor models or same model via three different routers.
antipattern.reason: Vendor outage takes the whole chain down. Same model also shares content-policy refusal classes.

section.p: Symptom: chain failure during a vendor incident. Root cause: insufficient diversity. Fix: ensure at least 2 distinct providers; validate via the validator script.

antipattern.description: Gateway retries the chain on 400/401/422 client errors.
antipattern.reason: The error is the same on every provider; silent retry masks the bug and inflates cost.

section.p: Symptom: every call to a misconfigured endpoint runs all fallback providers before failing. Root cause: blanket retry policy. Fix: configure the gateway / app code to surface 4xx without retry; keep 5xx/429/timeout in the retry set.

antipattern.description: Routing prompt-caching workloads through a hosted gateway.
antipattern.reason: Most gateways do not pass cache-control headers correctly; the 90% cache discount evaporates and the gateway "saving" is dwarfed by the cache loss.

section.p: Symptom: prompt-caching cost increases after gateway adoption. Root cause: cache headers stripped. Fix: call vendor SDK directly for cached workloads; keep gateway for non-cached paths.

antipattern.description: Routing EU-resident user data through a hosted gateway in the US.
antipattern.reason: Adds a third-party data processor outside the residency boundary; GDPR / SCC obligations escape audit scope.

section.p: Symptom: DPO objection during compliance review. Root cause: gateway provider in wrong jurisdiction. Fix: self-host LiteLLM proxy inside the EU boundary, OR call vendor SDK directly with app-level fallback.
